sentences.text: They were scared, except my father.

Funny enough, he wasn't scared because in his appearance-- you know, this is the usual joke or put down-- like somebody says he looks Jewish or doesn't look Jewish, but I'm going to use this just for-- my father didn't look Jewish at all.
He had blue or green eyes, and he had white hair.
He was very premature of white.
And he had a straight nose.
You can-- you saw pictures.
Any of it, you will see later on.
So we moved around quite good.
A matter of fact, I do recall that he brought some bread back-- bread and some provisions back from the German troops that he met somewhere.
They thought he [INAUDIBLE]-- because of his-- of his way that he spoke, they thought he was a-- he was a Kraut.
In Austria, that was after the war started.
Oh yes, after the invasion.
That's right.
And, uh--
No, no.
Not-- not even-- no way.
No way.
No-- as far as I-- as far as I remember, there was not because first off, you had no relatives in the United States or anywhere, you know.
I learned to be a tailor.
I worked for a tailor, but I didn't learn to be a tailor.
I washed the diapers, and I-- I cleaned.
I was the babysitter.
This is-- when you became an apprentice, that-- that's-- that was the usual routine, even in Austria.
First you cleaned up the house and the joint down there.
And, uh, after a while, he tried to show you how to do what you were supposed to do   in the first place.
In other words, so I knew how to handle a needle, [? and made ?] a thimble one, and-- and to saw.
And he only gave one job, and it's doing lapels in the inside with the lining in there and making stitches and all that kind of thing.
And, uh, that's it.
Thats's-- that's what I did.
I didn't get paid for it either.
Well, they were-- they were, uh-- there was always a Jewish organization that took care of certain expenses.
That was it, because we had no money.
We had no money-- none, literally no money, what's-- period.
Jewelers, whatever jewelry-- which wouldn't have lasted anyhow-- that my mother had was taken away from her, or given away by her when we crossed the border to the smugglers, and so that was it.
There was nothing.
And my sister certainly couldn't send any money from England, because she-- I think she made a pound a month or something like that, uh, plus room and board, so.
We weren't th-- look, I was never hungry.
Let's put it this way.
However my father did it, he provided food for the table.
We didn't have geese, and we didn't have chicken, and didn't have steak, uh, but whatever.
And I made a lot of friends down there.
Uh, you know, I ate in other homes of boys that were Jewish that were from Belgium.
So I used to be invited for-- for dinner, which was fabulous.
I loved it.
At the time, nothing, not very much.
We had to wear stars.
We had to wear stars.
And, uh, that's what got me into trouble when I was arrested and sent to Malines.
I was in a-- in a  movie house with a girl and, uh, you know, didn't wear my star.
My paper said that I was Jewish, and, uh, that was the end of that.
But, uh--
Yeah, in a place called Kwaadmechelen.
But that-- I-- [SIGHS] it was forced labor in-- in-- in a very soft way.
Lots of food, uh, freedom, uh, to walk around in the village, because Kwaadmechelen is a village.
Uh, there was a curfew.
We couldn't go-- after a certain time, you couldn't leave your house.
Uh, the only so-called guards was, uh-- I don't know how many people were there.
It was 50, or 25, or whatever it was, usually families, OK?
There was one-- one-- one or two people from the gendarmerie, and that was it.
They didn't mistreat us.
It was just the opposite.
They just, uh--
Oh, yeah, sure.
I was there with my parents.
Uh, 1900-- and, uh, let me see now.
That was 1941.
Oh yeah, sure.
Well, Malines was horrible.
Malines was the first taste of-- of being guarded by-- there were dogs down there.
There was SS down there.
Uh, there were only men down there at the time that I was there, uh, which changed later on, because when the transport started in 1942, whole family came in.
This was just like the, uh, the central point of-- of getting people together down there to send them somewhere else, which happened to be Auschwitz.
And, uh-- but I learned a lesson.
Have you ever heard an ex-- expression to make yourself invisible, while looking at somebody and somebody looking at you?
I developed it to an art.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's like one says, keep your nose clean.
It's not really the same thing, but I, uh-- in German, you say-- you use the word [GERMAN], in other words, to be seen.
Well, I choose to be-- not to be seen.
Uh, I've never volunteered for anything.

Uh, if I was asked to-- to clean the toilet, I clean the toilet, which was sometimes-- that doesn't mean I was submittive-- submissive, but I said to myself, I going to get my own somewhere else, sometime, somewhere.
Which will-- I will tell you later on what we did in Auschwitz.
If this is supposed to be, uh, uh, sabotage of some sort, well, let's say it was.
But, uh, uh, I wasn't-- I didn't act like a big shot, and I was very stubborn.
I still am.
And I have a short temper, and I still have.
Uh, but I tried to control it, and I think that this control, without knowing it, helped me a lot to go through many, many, many things.
That's right.
--to be invisible.
That's right.
That's right.
That's what it is.
I mean, I know this is very ambiguous, that word, but-- but it can be done.
I mean, the p-- though I think at the most important factor about being invisible is to be lucky.
No, you know?
It's-- you know people usually make certain moves and they say, ah.
It is.
That's the most-- I think the most important factor of all these things is luck.
It's the-- the-- the-- the throw of a dice.
It's, uh, it's-- it's what's going to happen to you.

Uh, if you-- if you're resigned that-- uh, and this is my biggest beef when I talk about the religious part of what happened, and they say my beliefs is that people simply write it off by saying it is written.
Bullshit.
It isn't written.
Nothing's written.
I resent that.
If you're lucky, something you can't help.
Nothing is written.
I do not believe in that-- that somebody has mapped something out, that I'm going to live until I'm 74, or 73, or I'm going to die when I'm six months old.
I don't believe in it.
It's something that happens.
It's like the-- like an evolution.
And-- and-- and-- and I have had discussions-- not arguments.
I never argue about the religious things.
With people that say it is written, I said, what's written?
It's written that a-- that a-- that a six week or three-month-old baby is going to go into the gas chamber.
Then where's it written?
And-- and if-- if it is so, then whoever wrote that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then I stop right there, because I'm not-- I'm not trying to be sacrilegious.
Doesn't make-- to me, it doesn't make sense.
What the hell is written?
If you're lucky, your luck, fine, that's it.
Anyhow, let's go to the next step down there.
He-- How long were you in Malines?
In Malines, until-- uh, the-- what is it? 25th of, uh, mm, August 1942.
They started sending transports out before me.
I think my transport was number five.
And, uh--
Families, yeah, they were coming in.
And, uh--
No.
No.
Uh, I, uh, uh, I was-- my transport was number five.
And as a matter of fact, uh, uh, we were just picked out.
So I think that I tried to avoid a lot of things that happened, but I-- well, I couldn't.
Anyhow, I, uh, know that we had-- my transport number five was nine.
And this is exact, because of the, uh, proof-- I didn't have it at the time-- what that-- Klarsfeld?
Is that?
Yeah, that's him, Serge.
Uh, you know, he brought out a book that he got from the archives, uh, in Belgium.
Our transport was-- were 995 people, uh, women, men, children, old people, and  so on, so on.
When we arrived in Auschwitz on the 27th-- two days later-- of August, of, uh, this, 780 people went in the gas chamber.
Uh, transport chain.
It was, uh, not-- not a cattle car, no cattle cars.
No, no.
These were-- these were regular from Belgium, and even from Holland, [? they traveled. ?] I worked in Kanada.
I saw it coming.
They didn't have any cattle cars down there.
Later on, apparently, they had.
But when-- my train was just an ordinary train, that's all.
And, uh--
Ah, not a clue.
Not a clue.
Nothing.
And, uh--
I don't know.
I don't remember.
I swear, I don't remember.
I haven't got a clue.
Nothing.
I mean, I'm not going to, uh, uh, uh, fabricate thi-- I haven't got a clue.
Nothing.
I do not know.
But as I said before, out of the 995, 780 were gassed and 26 got into the camp.
Now, I don't the, uh, the proportion of, uh, of men and women.
Although, in his-- in his, uh, files, or in his books that he has, he gives you a distinction of how many men went in, how many women went in.
And, uh, uh, out of these, uh, returnees, as I call them, uh, the 26 were freed in, uh, 1945.
Um, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there were two people, two guys.
One, I knew from Vienna that was in the same transport that I was.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, uh, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] one is dead, and the other one is still alive.
Um, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Do you want me to start from the beginning?
So we got out of the train.
There was screaming and shouting down the end.
We had people that we saw that were running around in striped uniforms that I knew already from-- this I remember from-- from Buchenwald.
That's for sure.
With their full uniform, you know, their jacket and their pants and, uh, and, uh, and their-- their beret.
And, uh, a lot of SS with dogs, and with-- with rifles and with the pistols, and all that kind of horse manure.
And, uh, we lined up, uh, the women to the left, and with the children, and the men, all men, to the right.
And then we went through the routine of the selection, where the physician-- if I can use-- I shouldn't use that word because he was just a butcher-- was standing there.
And when I came up and he asked me what, uh, what is your-- I was 18 years old.
What's your profession?
I'm a tailor.
Go-- pointed to the right, OK?
And as I say, there was only-- there were only 26 people in total.
I don't know how many women.
I don't know how many we were, if we were 15 or 20 guys, or-- or-- or 16 guys that went.
I don't remember that anymore.
And we marched into Auschwitz.
And so the first thing that I saw down there in Auschwitz was, uh, the sign, which is famous, 'Arbeit Macht Frei.' And I don't think that I have to translate that.
And, uh, so we marched in, and then I-- I-- I saw a sight that-- that-- stays with me until today.
Gee, ?] it was-- it was early in the morning.
And I saw a bunch of block buildings, you know, like brick buildings.
They weren't barracks.
This was Auschwitz One, the main camp.

And, uh, I saw some characters that were-- that looked extremely healthy, very muscular, in, uh, in-- in uniforms of, uh-- at that time, I didn't know what uniform it was, but it wasn't a prison uniform.
They were Russian soldiers.
There weren't too many, because they killed all the Russian soldiers and kept the minimum down there.
I guess if the Red Cross came in, they want to show them that they still had some Russian prisoners.
And some other Polish speaking or Ukrainian speaking guys that pushed a cart.
Uh, the cart must have been about, [SIGHS] I don't know, eight, nine meters long, with very, very huge, four huge wheels.
And, uh, in the front, there weren't horses.
There were people that are steering [INAUDIBLE].
And they stopped there in front of a block, and all of a sudden, bodies came shooting out.
Later on, I learned that they were called the harvester commando, [GERMAN] kommando.
They were the people that picked up the bodies that either were killed during the night or died during the night, and were laying down there.
And they picked them up, and then they put them to an assembly place, where they, later on, they're picked up on a truck and brought to the [INAUDIBLE].
I-- I-- I-- I-- I-- I did not recall this at the time.
This would be an untruth, but when I came out, you know that-- there was a turmoil in me when I came out because you're free.
What does freedom mean?
I don't know what it means.
Freedom means that all of a sudden, I have all the food that I want.
And then certain things started to appear in my head, and I remembered that scenario that I saw when I walked in.
And I recalled that-- I think I was 12 years old.
My father always gave me books.
He was a bookworm.
He wanted me to read a lot.
And I read a story by-- and-- and-- and that I remember very clearly-- by Alighieri.
He was, uh, an Italian poet, and he wrote a book.

And he wrote something about And, you know, when I-- when I [INAUDIBLE] it, and I said to myself, if was bad, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and look at this, it must've been a picnic to be in because all he described is that the people working down there with-- with no shirt on, just pants.
And they're shoveling coal in intense heat, and that's hell.
This is worse than hell.
And so we, uh-- we, uh-- we went down there, and we went to, uh, uh, eh, appellplatz, where people used to be counted sometimes.
There were executions were [? placed. ?] And then we had to, uh-- we walked into a building.
We dropped all our clothes.
And, uh, our heads were shorn, and, uh, we went, uh, through it at a table down there.
Oh, we received, uh, two pieces of cloth, uh, which had a number on there, which is my number, 62 oh 22, and, uh, the insignia, that, uh-- the Magen David, red and yellow.
And we went through the t-- and we were numb.
You understand what I mean?
I mean, there was nothing there.
What impression?
What?
Numb.
Completely empty.
Maybe it was just me, but I think it affected everybody.
We were thirsty.
And the first thing we heard, don't drink.
You want to die?
Drink.
What do you mean you want to die?
Don't drink.
So don't drink.
It's a command.
Don't drink, so we don't drink.
So here, this guy puts the number on there, tattoos the number on there, and then we go to the shower.
It was the first and last shower I had in, I think, about four months.
Blistering hot.
And so we got out, and, uh, we were assigned to, uh, certain parts to where we marched.
So I went into the barrack number one, which was the-- where the tailor department was.
What's the tailor department?
The tailor department was what when you arrived and you got that number, you got your tattoo, and you got your clothing, you went there with all these things to number one down there.
And there was a little-- not a desk-- a-- a-- a little anteroom with a desk-like thing, a piece of board.
And there was a guy standing behind it, and you gave him your clothing.
You gave him your number, and he gave it to somebody behind him.
There were about 10 of 12 machine operators down there, and they sewed on-- sewed a number on there, then gave it back, and then you went to somewhere else.
So I stayed there.
So I'm a tailor, but I don't know anything about a sewing machine.
What skills [? they're ?] going to show?
So I-- the kapo was Jewish.
And I said to him, I said, it's OK.
You speak German?
I said, yeah.
Perfect.
He said, OK, we need a guy like you around.
OK, I'm going ahead.
So I worked there for a few weeks-- actually, more than a month.
Well, you mentioned that, you know, you identified yourself as speaking German.
Now how did this affect your treatment?
It didn't.
It didn't at all.
I don't know why he said that because, uh, he was-- he was-- I think he came-- where did he come-- he came from France.
And he spoke Yiddish and French, and he spoke a little bit of German that he probably picked up down there.
That's all he said.
I don't know why.
Oh, that was-- that was-- that's-- that's coming a little later.
That's-- that's just the working part.
Now comes the other part.
The other part was horrible.
Uh, so, uh, the first night, I'm in the barracks down there, uh, you know.
I walk in.
People are having their bunks and this and that.
And somebody comes up to me and talks to me.
I don't understand what he's saying, nothing, not a word.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He spoke Yiddish, and I didn't understand a word of Yiddish.
I didn't know any Yiddish.
And I answered in German, and then I heard the expression of-- that I didn't know either, [YIDDISH].
And then he added something with a-- [YIDDISH].
I forgot.
Something with coffee.
Nobody spoke to me.
I'm desolated.
I want to talk to somebody.
I want to-- nobody speaks to me.
I come up, they turn their heads away.
As a matter of fact, a guy kicked me in-- in-- in-- in my shins.
I want to tell him, what did I do?
Two nights later, I'm laying on my bunk, and I cry.
And there's an old man next to me.
He must have been all of 48 years old, real old man.
And he says, hey, who's-- later on, I understood what he said.
He said, why are you crying, in Yiddish.
So I started talking, and he speaks half Yiddish, half German.
And he tells me that he is-- he is an Austrian.
He's talking about 1918, the monarchy.
And he starts to explain to me.
He said, they don't-- they don't speak to you because you don't speak Yiddish, and they don't trust you.
And you're a [YIDDISH].
And I said, what is this?

So he explains to me in as short as he can that when the people-- the Polish Jews-- came to Austria in 1914, '15, '16, '18, whatever, '19, '20, they were looked upon by the so-called old Austrians-- which were the first, the second, or third generation-- as dirty and unedu-- uneducated and filthy.
And they were nothing, and they're this and that.
And I said, so I [? have ?] [? to? ?] I said, I don't know about that.
I said I never saw or heard, when I grew up, that this guy was from here.
And we had so many Czechs down there.
There was no difference.
This was Hans and Josef and [?
Karly ?] and who knows what, [INAUDIBLE].
Anyhow, what shall I do, I said to the guy.
I said, you have to-- how did he say?
Du musst lernen mamaloshen.
You have to learn mamaloshen.
Now I don't know what is mamaloshen.
He says, Yiddish.
How do you learn the language?
He said, that's very simple.
He said, you have to learn how to swear.
I said, swear what?
He said, just make up words.
And he gave me a couple of samples, which I used first, and then I elaborate.
Four weeks later, I was perfect.
I was with the in crowds.
Everybody talked to me.
I could converse with them very badly, but I could understood more than I did before.
And that was my first experience.
And, uh, you know, when I talked about with people, and it's not-- you know, it's like a cliche, my best friends.
My best friends came from Poland and from Russia, but they were my friends.
There was nobody else.
And I talked to them about it.
He said, my father used to tell me, too.
I said, the German and the Austrian Jews used to treat the Poles like dirt.
And I said, that's what happened to them.
That's they way they were brought up.
That was Auschwitz One.
[INAUDIBLE]
Until about December.
So, uh, one day, uh, transport arrived from, uh-- I don't know.
It was from Belgium or from Holland.
And here's this guy that I went to school with, same age as me.
And he comes up, and he gives me the uniform.
And we weren't supposed to talk to anybody down there.
And really [? short, ?] he asked me, you know, he said, my father is in here?
Where's my father?
My sister?
And there was a window out there, and I pointed.
And I said, see that smoke coming up down there?
Very callously.
This is-- this is really what they tried to do with us.
They-- they tried to take not only our dignity, they wanted to make us into animals, and very often they succeeded-- not completely, but in part they did.
And I said, that's where they are, just dancing.
Where did I pick this word up?
I picked it up from somebody else.
It's a smoke dance.
I said, that's where they come.
The guy gets into hysterics.
I mean, he-- first, I thought he was going to strangle me.
And there's a whole commotion.
Everybody comes-- the block elders, the-- the kapo comes.
The SS man that was in charge of the block outside that used to take the count of the role call, you know, he comes running in.
First, I get beaten up.
Beat the shit out of me.
And when they find out from this guy, they want to quiet him down, you know?
Everything-- everything had to be-- di-- diplomacy had to be there at all times.
This is a lie.
It's not true.
You're probably going to meet him again.
They want that guy-- who knows what that guy was intending to do?
And I was thrown down in the cellar.
The next day there was a trial.
Before the trial was, uh, I got what they used to call [GERMAN], which means a flogging, 25 on my behind.
That was the number one thing before the trial even started.
There was a contraption that they had down there with you where you had to lay down.
They took your pants down.
And you laid down straight with your head down there and with you feet down there with like a 45 degree angle.
And your feet were in a box on the bottom, so you can't take it out.
And somebody stood there and held your head, and the guy gave it to you.
And you had to count.
If you lost count, he started over again.
Look, I'm not macho.
It's just something that happened.
And again, I can only say one thing-- I was lucky because I received it, once again, at a later date from another source.
And so my sentence was, uh, three months in the Straf company, the SK, in the penal colony, or the penal kommando in Birkenau.
I didn't even know that Birkenau existed at the time.
Although, when we marched from the, uh-- from the-- the ramp when we came in to Auschwitz, we saw two camps, one on the left, one on the right.
One was a women's camp, and one was-- this was in Birkenau.
We had to march through Birkenau to get in there.
And we marched there for the-- we were  waiting on the door, on the gate.
That's where they brought me, and there were, I think, three or four other guys.
And we marched off.
We went to Birkenau.
Oh, yeah.
At that time I did, of course.
One day.
This is-- this is why I say they wanted to, uh, not only to take our dignity away, they also made us into animals.
And it was quite well known.
You see, there was a saying down there.
They used to say that, uh, uh, Germans may die-- meaning prisoners.
Poles will die.
Jews must die.
There are certain sentences that were given to you.
It was just like the ABC in school.
You learned it.
All the guy used to say, how long you think you're going to last?
Two days?
Three days?
You see that?
Everybody always pointed against the sky down there where you see the sort of chimney.
That's where you're going to get out, down there.
Uh, and, uh, again, to, uh, to keep this whole thing in perspective, uh, how I handled it, I don't know.
It would be stupid.
I-- I-- I can't access it right now, and I can't, uh, take it apart.
Uh, it just happened, and it was there.
And that's the way it was.
And so we arrived at the Straf company.
And, uh, I found out something 10 seconds after I got in there.
The Straf company was right within the camp of Birkenau, but there was a wall around it with a door to go in, OK?
And there was a courtyard.
Straf company, eh, we were supposed to last two days, three days at the most.
I lasted for almost a month.
By luck-- not only by sheer luck, but, uh, I also had somebody to protect me.
And funny enough at that time, in retrospect, I did not think of my grandfather.
I got help from somebody else.
I got help from the two guys that arrived with me from Belgium that used to work in Kanada.
Story. ?] Anyhow, I-- down there, we had a Kommando Fuhrer, which was the man in charge of the, uh, thing.
And we walked out every morning.
And, uh, the first morning, we walked out, and there was an irrigation to be done down there.
And I saw the first three killings of Mr.
Josef Eckhart, Unterscharfuhrer.
Uh, he threw them in down there, and he stomped at him with his boots until they drowned.
And they took him out.
Another thing that was very, very [? insignificant ?] is that, uh, dead people, any dead person, was referred to as pieces, [GERMAN].
In other words, when the guy says [GERMAN] meant 10 dead people.
Very common term, OK?
And did it matter if they were Jews or not?
That didn't make any.
It was a dead person, that's all.
Um--
